definition: Six characteristics that define a character: Strength, Dexterity, Constitution, Intelligence, Wisdom, and Charisma. Each ranges from 3-18 with a modifier from -4 to +4.
details:
Roll 3d6 in order for each of the six stats: Strength, Dexterity, Constitution, Intelligence, Wisdom, Charisma. Note the total and modifier for each.

If none of your stats are 14 or higher, you may optionally roll a completely new set of six numbers.

StatModifier
1-3-4
4-5-3
6-7-2
8-9-1
10-110
12-13+1
14-15+2
16-17+3
18++4

definition: Physical power. Used for melee attacks, smashing doors, bending bars, and lifting heavy objects.
Agility and reflexes. Used for ranged attacks, dodging traps, climbing, stealth, and initiative.
Endurance and resistance to injury. Used for holding breath, resisting poison, and death timer calculations.
Logical ability. Used for wizard spellcasting, knowledge checks, navigation, and magical research.
Instinct and willpower. Used for priest spellcasting, perception, tracking, and sensing danger.
Appeal and presence. Used for social influence, NPC reactions, and certain class abilities.

details:
Your stat modifier is derived from your stat score and applies to all checks, attacks, and damage rolls using that stat.

Stat ScoreModifier
1-3-4
4-5-3
6-7-2
8-9-1
10-110
12-13+1
14-15+2
16-17+3
18++4

definition: Your character's species/race (Dwarf, Elf, Goblin, Half-Orc, Halfling, Human). Each ancestry grants unique abilities.
Your character's profession and adventuring role. Core classes: Fighter, Priest, Thief, Wizard. Each determines hit points, weapons, armor, and special abilities.
Your character's former occupation before adventuring. Grants a free talent related to that profession and helps define your character's history.
A character's moral compass: Lawful (order, justice), Neutral (balance, pragmatism), or Chaotic (freedom, destruction). Affects deity choice and NPC interactions.
details:
Alignment defines your role in the clash between good and evil. All creatures are connected to the eternal conflict waged by Law, Chaos, and Neutrality.

  • Lawful: Benevolence, fairness, order, and virtue. Lawful characters operate from a "good of the whole" mentality.
  • Chaotic: Destruction, ambition, and wickedness. Chaotic characters adopt a "survival of the fittest" mentality.
  • Neutral: Balance between Law and Chaos. Neutral characters align with the cycle of growth and decline, adopting a "nature must take its course" mentality.

Your alignment affects your deity choice and your class title progression.

definition: A measure of how much damage a character can sustain. Reduced by damage, restored by resting. At 0 HP, the death timer begins.
How hard a character is to hit. Calculated from armor worn plus DEX modifier (unless wearing plate). Attacks must meet or exceed AC to hit.
details:
Your armor class (AC) is 10 + your Dexterity modifier. Wearing armor changes your AC.

ArmorCostSlotsACProperties
Leather armor10 gp111 + DEX mod—
Chainmail60 gp213 + DEX modDisadv. on stealth, swim
Plate mail130 gp315No swim, disadv. stealth
Shield10 gp1+2Occupies one hand
Mithral (metal only)×4-1—No stealth/swim penalty

You can wear the types of armor listed for your class. 0-level PCs can wear all armor until 1st level. Attacks must meet or exceed your AC to hit.

definition: The carrying capacity system. A character has a number of gear slots equal to their Strength score. Each item occupies one or more slots.
details:
You can carry a number of items equal to your Strength stat or 10, whichever is higher. Unless noted, all gear besides typical clothing fills one gear slot.

ItemQty per Slot
Arrows / Crossbow bolts1-20
Backpack1 (first one free)
Coins100 (first 100 free)
Gems1-10
Iron spikes1-10
Rations1-3
All other gear1 per slot

Gear that is hard to transport might fill more than one slot. Two-handed weapons and heavy armor typically take 2-3 slots.

definition: Characters advance by earning XP from treasure, not from killing monsters. Each level requires meeting an XP target and passing a talent check.
details:
Characters advance by earning XP from treasure and boons, not from killing monsters.

To gain a level, you need to earn current level x 10 XP. Once you reach a new level, your total XP resets to zero.

LevelTalent RollLevel Up At...
1+110 XP
2—20 XP
3+130 XP
4—40 XP
5+150 XP
6—60 XP
7+170 XP
8—80 XP
9+190 XP
10—100 XP

On leveling up: gain a new title, roll your class's HP die and add to max HP. At talent roll levels, roll once on your class talent table (duplicates stack unless noted).

definition: As characters gain levels, they earn titles reflecting their growing fame. Titles differ by class and alignment.
Characters know Common plus languages from their ancestry and class. Rare languages (Celestial, Diabolic, Draconic, Primordial) require special training or class features.
New characters roll for starting gear or use a crawling kit. Gear is tracked in gear slots and includes weapons, armor, and adventuring supplies.
At the start of combat, everyone rolls d20 + DEX modifier. The GM uses the highest DEX mod among monsters. Highest result goes first, then clockwise.
details:
At the beginning of the game (or when combat starts), everyone rolls a d20 + DEX modifier. The GM uses the highest DEX modifier of any monsters.

The person with the highest result takes the first turn. Turns go clockwise from that person.

Surprise: The GM determines if any creatures are unaware of each other. A creature who surprises another takes one full turn before a new initiative order is rolled.

Freeform Mode: Some GMs keep a loose round-robin, letting players decide their turn order and actions before circling back to the GM's turn.

definition: One full cycle where every participant takes one turn. Each turn allows one action plus movement up to near distance.
details:
A combat round completes when each person has taken one turn. During combat:

  • Characters act in initiative order (clockwise from highest)
  • The GM takes actions for monsters and environmental effects
  • Timers count down at the start of each character's turn

Player Turn:

  1. Count down any personal timers (spells, effects)
  2. Take one action and move up to near (can split movement). Move near again if skipping your action.
  3. GM describes results

GM Turn:

  1. Count down any timers not tracked by players
  2. Check for random encounters if needed
  3. Take actions/movements for creatures and environmental effects
  4. Describe what characters notice

definition: On your turn: move up to near (30 ft), take one action (attack, cast spell, use item, etc.). You can split movement before and after your action. Move near again if you skip your action.
Roll d20 + STR modifier + bonuses vs target's AC. Uses melee weapons at close range (5 ft).
Roll d20 + DEX modifier + bonuses vs target's AC. Uses ranged weapons (bows, crossbows) or thrown weapons.
When you hit, roll your weapon's damage dice + relevant bonuses. The GM subtracts that from the target's HP.
details:
When you hit a target with an attack or spell, roll your weapon or spell's damage dice + relevant bonuses. The GM subtracts that from the target's HP.

WeaponDamageProperties
Club / Dagger / Shortbow1d4—
Mace / Crossbow / Javelin1d6—
Longsword / Longbow1d8—
Bastard sword / Greataxe1d8/1d10Versatile
Greatsword1d12Two-handed

definition: A natural 20 on an attack roll. For weapons: double the damage dice. For spells: double one numerical effect.
details:
You deal a critical hit if you roll a natural 20 on an attack roll or spellcasting check.

  • Weapon critical: Double the weapon's damage dice on the attack (then add modifiers normally).
  • Spell critical: You may double one of the spell's numerical effects. On a focus spell, this remains in effect until your next focus check.

definition: A natural 1 on an attack roll is always a miss, regardless of bonuses. On spellcasting, it triggers a critical failure.
Attacking a creature behind half cover has disadvantage. Full cover blocks targeting entirely. Difficult terrain halves movement.
When reducing a creature to 0 HP, you can choose to knock it unconscious instead of killing it.
When a monster group is reduced to half, the GM rolls a Wisdom check (DC 15) for the monsters. On failure, they flee or surrender.
If wielding two one-handed weapons, you can attack with both on your turn. The second attack has disadvantage.
On your turn you can: attack, cast a spell, use an item, dash (move near again), hide, help an ally, shove, grapple, or attempt any creative action.
details:
Actions you can take on your turn in combat:

  • Melee Attack: Roll 1d20 + STR mod + bonuses. Hit if total >= target's AC.
  • Ranged Attack: Roll 1d20 + DEX mod + bonuses. Hit if total >= target's AC.
  • Cast a Spell: Takes one action (see Spellcasting).
  • Improvise: Any creative action — swinging on a vine, kicking over a table. GM may require a stat check.
  • Multitask: Small parallel tasks (standing up, speaking, drinking a potion, activating a magic item) don't typically use your action.

Movement: Move up to near (30 ft) on your turn, split however you want. Move near again if you skip your action.

Cover/Terrain: Attacking a creature behind half-cover has disadvantage. Full cover blocks targeting. Difficult terrain costs double movement.

definition: When a PC drops to 0 HP, roll 1d4 + CON modifier (minimum 1). This is how many rounds before the PC dies.
details:
A character who goes to 0 HP falls unconscious and is dying.

Death Timer: On their turn, a dying character rolls 1d4 + CON modifier (minimum 1 total). They die in that many rounds unless healed or stabilized.

On each subsequent turn, the dying player rolls a d20:

  • Natural 20: The character rises with 1 HP
  • Any other result: Nothing happens, the timer continues to tick down

Stabilize: An intelligent being can give first aid at close range. On a successful DC 15 INT check, the target stops dying but remains unconscious. They need rest to recover.

Healing: A character who goes above 0 HP wakes up and is no longer dying.

definition: The DM rolls the death timer in secret instead of the player, adding dramatic tension.
On each turn while dying, the PC rolls a d20. On a natural 20, the PC rises with 1 HP.
A PC at close range to a downed character can make a DC 15 INT check. On success, the target stops dying but remains unconscious.
When the death timer expires, the character dies. Dead characters can sometimes be raised by powerful magic.
The default mode of play when not in combat. Characters explore, talk, and interact with the environment in turn order.
details:
Characters are in crawling rounds while not in combat. They are exploring, talking, and engaging with the environment.

During crawling rounds, characters can take actions such as:

  • Prying a gem from a statue
  • Sneaking up on a slumbering manticore
  • Tapping on a suspicious wall for hidden doors
  • Scanning a room for signs of hidden enemies
  • Giving a rousing speech to fearful townsfolk

The GM can allow players to regroup during crawling rounds. PCs within reasonable reach can come together into a marching order and move as a group.

Random Encounters: The GM checks for random encounters during crawling rounds based on the environment's danger level.

definition: Any place where darkness, danger, and myth reign supreme — crumbling ruins, ancient strongholds, mysterious towers, gloomy forests, haunted caves.
All characters need light to see. Any area outside a light source's illumination is in total darkness. Darkness-adapted creatures (monsters) can see without light.
details:
All characters need light to see, but darkness-adapted creatures of the Shadowdark do not.

Any area outside of a light source's illumination is in total darkness.

Total Darkness Effects:

  • Creatures not adapted to darkness have disadvantage on all tasks requiring sight
  • The environment becomes deadly — the GM checks for a random encounter every crawling round

Darkvision or similar abilities let certain creatures (and some ancestries/classes with special talents) see in darkness without penalty.

definition: Torches last 1 hour of real time and illuminate near distance. Lanterns last 1 hour per oil flask. If real time can't be tracked, 1 hour = 10 rounds.
details:
Most light sources last for up to one hour of real time and illuminate a limited area. If you can't track real time, assume 1 hour = 10 rounds.

SourceRangeDurationNotes
TorchNear (30 ft)1 hour real time5 sp, 1 slot
LanternDouble near (60 ft)1 hour per oil flask5 gp, has shutter
Campfire (3 torches)Near (30 ft)Up to 8 hoursCan't be moved; needs 1 PC near

Multiple light sources: When lighting a new source, either (1) the new source "rides along" on the current timer, or (2) extinguish all old sources and start a new timer.

Total Darkness: Creatures not adapted to darkness have disadvantage on all tasks requiring sight. The GM checks for a random encounter every crawling round.

definition: Time passes in the game world at the same pace as real time. One real minute = one game minute. Critical for tracking torch duration.
Close = 5 feet. Near = up to 30 feet. Far = within sight during an encounter or scene.
details:
Distances are abstracted into three ranges:

RangeDistanceExamples
Close5 feetMelee range, first aid, picking a lock
NearUp to 30 feetStandard move per turn, torch light range
FarWithin sightLongbow range, far spells

Characters can move near on their turn (can split movement before and after action). Move near again if skipping action. Difficult terrain costs double movement.

definition: STR or DEX check to climb at half speed. Falling if you fail by 5+ points.
1d6 damage per 10 feet fallen.
Swim at half speed (STR check in rough water). Hold breath for rounds equal to CON mod (min 1). Then CON check each round or take 1d6 damage.
details:
Characters swim at half speed. In rough water, a STR check is required.

Holding Breath: You can hold your breath for a number of rounds equal to your CON modifier (minimum 1).

After that, you must make a CON check each round or take 1d6 damage per round until you exit the hazard.

Armor Penalties:

  • Chainmail: Disadvantage on swimming
  • Plate mail: Cannot swim at all
  • Mithral: Removes swimming penalty from metal armor

definition: During crawling rounds, the GM can allow players to regroup into marching order and move as a group.
Roll 1d6 periodically. On a 1, a random encounter occurs. Frequency: Unsafe = every 3 rounds, Risky = every 2, Deadly = every round.
When an encounter starts, roll d6: 1 = Close, 2-4 = Near, 5-6 = Far.
Roll 2d6 for what encountered creatures are doing: 2-4 Hunting, 5-6 Eating, 7-8 Building/nesting, 9-10 Socializing, 11 Guarding, 12 Sleeping.
Roll 2d6 + CHA mod to determine NPC attitude: 0-6 Hostile, 7-8 Suspicious, 9 Neutral, 10-11 Curious, 12+ Friendly.
The GM determines if creatures are unaware of each other. A surprising creature takes one free turn before new initiative is rolled.
DEX checks to go undetected. Can't hide while visible. Looking in the right place auto-reveals. Otherwise, searcher makes a check.
Roll d20 + INT mod (wizards) or d20 + WIS mod (priests) vs the spell's DC (10 + spell tier). Meet or beat to cast successfully.
details:
To cast a spell, make a spellcasting check: roll 1d20 + your spellcasting stat modifier (INT for wizards, WIS for priests).

The DC equals 10 + the spell's tier.

Spell TierDC
Tier 111
Tier 212
Tier 313
Tier 414
Tier 515

Success: The spell takes effect.

Failure: The spell does not take effect. You can try again on a future turn.

Natural 20 (Critical): Double one of the spell's numerical effects.

Natural 1 (Critical Failure): The spell fails. Wizard: Lose that spell until you rest + roll on the Wizard Mishap table. Priest: Lose that spell until you complete penance + rest.

definition: Spells are classified in tiers 1-5, with higher tiers being more powerful. The DC to cast = 10 + tier.
Close (5 ft), Near (30 ft), Far (within sight), or Self. Determines how far the spell can reach.
How long spell effects last: Instant, rounds, focus, or longer durations.
Last as long as you concentrate. Can't cast other focus spells while focusing. Each round, make a DC 9 + spell tier spellcasting check to maintain. Ends on failure or natural 1.
Same spell on same target doesn't stack. The most powerful effect (e.g., longer duration) takes precedence.
Natural 20 on spellcasting check: double one numerical effect of the spell. On focus spells, this lasts until your next focus check.
details:
If you roll a natural 20 on your spellcasting check, you may double one of the spell's numerical effects.

Examples:

  • A healing spell that restores 1d8 HP could restore 2d8 HP
  • A spell that deals 2d6 damage could deal 4d6 damage
  • A spell that lasts 5 rounds could last 10 rounds

On a focus spell, the doubled effect remains in place until your next focus check.

definition: Natural 1: spell fails. Wizard spells can't be cast again until rest + roll on Wizard Mishap table. Priest spells can't be cast again until penance is completed.
When a wizard rolls natural 1, they lose the spell until rest and roll on the mishap table for their spell tier. Effects range from self-damage to permanent spell loss.
When a priest rolls natural 1, their deity revokes the spell. They must undertake penance (determined by the GM) before they can cast it again.
details:
When a priest rolls a natural 1 on a spellcasting check, their deity is greatly displeased and revokes that spell.

To regain the spell, the priest must:

  1. Complete penance appropriate to their deity and alignment
  2. Successfully complete a rest

Penance requires a holy quest, ritualistic atonement, or a material sacrifice that you donate or destroy:

Spell TierSacrifice Value
Tier 15 gp
Tier 220 gp
Tier 340 gp
Tier 490 gp
Tier 5150 gp

Warning: Inadequate or subversive penance (donating to a party member, etc.) makes the spell loss permanent.

definition: Contain a spell. Any spellcaster can attempt to cast if the spell is on their list. DC = 10 + tier. Writing disappears after use, whether successful or not.
details:
Scrolls contain magic spells. Spellcasters can use them to cast spells on their spell list, even if they don't know the spell.

To cast from a scroll: Succeed on a spellcasting check with DC = 10 + spell tier.

  • Success or Failure: The magical writing disappears and the scroll ceases to work (one use only)
  • Critical Failure: The scroll is consumed AND casters with mishap tables must roll a mishap

Failing to cast a spell from a scroll does not impact the ability to cast your known spells.

definition: Contain a spell. Spellcasters can attempt to cast if the spell is on their list. DC = 10 + tier. Failed = wand stops working until rest. Natural 1 = wand breaks permanently.
details:
Wands contain magic spells. Spellcasters can use them to cast spells on their spell list, even if they don't know the spell.

To cast from a wand: Succeed on a spellcasting check with DC = 10 + spell tier.

  • Failure: The wand stops working until you complete a rest
  • Critical Failure: The wand permanently breaks, and casters with mishap tables must roll a mishap

Failing to cast a spell from a wand does not impact the ability to cast your known spells.

definition: The number of spells a caster knows depends on their class and level, as shown in their class's Spells Known table.
An expanded penance system for priests. When spellcasting critically fails, roll on the Divine Penance table (d12) matching the spell's tier.
details:
When a priest critically fails a spellcasting check, their deity revokes that spell. The priest must complete ritualistic penance and a successful rest to regain it.

The GM determines the exact nature of the penance based on deity and alignment. Penance requires one of:

  • Holy Quest: A task aligned with the deity's values
  • Ritualistic Atonement: Prayer, fasting, or ceremony
  • Material Sacrifice: Donate or destroy valuables (see table below)
Spell TierSacrifice Value
Tier 15 gp
Tier 220 gp
Tier 340 gp
Tier 490 gp
Tier 5150 gp

Warning: Inadequate or subversive penance (such as donating your sacrifice to a party member) only displeases your deity further and makes the spell loss permanent.

definition: d12 effects include: Atonement (roll twice), Blasphemy (radiance damage), Humility (target self), Envy (target ally), Retribution (spell locked 1 week), Tithe (lose all coins), and more.
details:
For Tier 1-3 priest spells lost through critical failure, penance options:

  • Sacrifice: Destroy or donate valuables worth the spell tier's value (Tier 1: 5 gp, Tier 2: 20 gp, Tier 3: 40 gp)
  • Quest: Perform a task that furthers your deity's goals
  • Atonement: Ritual prayer or fasting appropriate to your faith

After completing penance, you must also successfully complete a rest to regain the spell.

Spell TierSacrifice Value
Tier 15 gp
Tier 220 gp
Tier 340 gp

definition: Harsher d12 effects include: Damnation (roll twice), Fury (AoE damage), Vow of Poverty (lose random gear), Vanity (lose CHA permanently), and worse.
details:
For Tier 4-5 priest spells lost through critical failure, penance is more demanding:

  • Sacrifice: Destroy or donate valuables worth the spell tier's value (Tier 4: 90 gp, Tier 5: 150 gp)
  • Major Quest: A significant holy mission befitting the power of the lost spell
  • Grand Atonement: Extended ritual or pilgrimage

After completing penance, you must also successfully complete a rest to regain the spell.

Spell TierSacrifice Value
Tier 490 gp
Tier 5150 gp

The GM has final say on whether penance is adequate. Subversive penance makes the loss permanent.

definition: Penance system for nature-based casters (Druids, Shamans). Roll on Nature's Penance table when spellcasting critically fails.
details:
Rangers, druids, and nature-aligned priests who critically fail may face Nature's Penance instead of standard divine penance.

Nature's penance typically involves:

  • Restore Balance: Heal a corrupted grove, free a trapped beast, or cleanse a tainted water source
  • Nature's Sacrifice: Return valuables to the earth (bury, cast into water, burn on a natural pyre)
  • Communion: Spend time in deep wilderness meditation

The sacrifice values are the same as standard divine penance. After completing penance, a successful rest is required.

definition: Sleep 8 hours and consume a ration. Restores all lost HP, recovers stat damage, and restores spent spell slots and abilities.
details:
To rest, a character must sleep for 8 hours and consume (or be fed) a ration. Sleep can be broken up for light tasks like taking watch.

Success: Regain all lost HP and recover any stat damage. Some talents, spells, or items also regain their uses.

Interruption: Each stressful interruption (including combat) requires a DC 12 CON check. On failure, you consume the ration but gain no benefit.

Resting in Danger: The GM checks for random encounters while resting in perilous environments:

  • Unsafe: Check every 3 hours
  • Risky: Check every 2 hours
  • Deadly: Check every hour

Campfire: Combine 3 torches into a campfire (can't be moved). Lasts up to 8 hours while at least one character stays near. Casts light to near distance.

definition: Each stressful interruption (including combat) requires a DC 12 CON check. Failure = ration consumed but no rest benefit.
Resting in dangerous areas risks interruption. Safe havens like towns allow uninterrupted rest. The GM determines danger level.
Characters earn XP from treasure quality, not from killing monsters. This encourages creative problem-solving over combat.
Poor = 0 XP (mundane items). Normal = 1 XP (bag of gold, gem, magic scroll). Fabulous = 3 XP (magic sword, giant diamond). Legendary = 10 XP (Staff of Ord, dragon hoard).
Death Timer expands categories: Poor (below 100 gp), Normal (100+ gp or consumable magic), Fabulous (permanent magic item or 1000+ gp), Legendary (artifact-level).
The amount of XP needed to level up. Increases with each level.
When leveling up, roll a d20 on your class talent table to gain a new ability.
Items take up gear slots. A character's total slots = STR score. Going over means encumbered (half speed, disadvantage on physical checks).
F = Finesse (use DEX instead of STR), Th = Thrown, V = Versatile (1-hand or 2-hand damage), 2H = Two-Handed, L = Loading.
Leather (AC 11+DEX), Chainmail (AC 13+DEX, stealth/swim disadvantage), Plate (AC 15, no swim, stealth disadvantage), Shield (+2 AC, one hand).
Magical metal armor variant. Costs 4x normal. Reduces gear slots by 1. Removes stealth and swim penalties.
A pre-built gear loadout for new characters: rope, torches, rations, and basic supplies.
A spellcaster using detect magic, after two rounds of focus, learns the item's name and general effects. DC 15 INT reveals all benefits.
Some magic items carry curses that aren't revealed until the item is used or identified. Curses persist until removed by specific means.
Roll the die twice and use the better result. Gained from favorable positioning, class abilities, or magical effects.
details:
When you have advantage, roll the same die twice and use the better result.

Examples: attacking from high ground, retrying a task you just failed (you now have insight), being a Dwarf rolling HP (Stout ancestry).

definition: Roll the die twice and use the worse result. Caused by unfavorable conditions, injuries, or environmental hazards.
details:
When you have disadvantage, roll the same die twice and use the worse result.

Examples: attacking while blinded, navigating while confused by poison, attacking a creature behind half-cover, being in chainmail while trying to swim or sneak.

definition: If you have both advantage and disadvantage on the same roll, they cancel out and you roll normally.
details:
If you have both advantage and disadvantage on a roll, they cancel each other out. You roll normally (once).

This applies regardless of how many sources of advantage or disadvantage you have — one of each cancels.

definition: An automatic success on any d20 roll, regardless of modifiers or DC.
An automatic failure on any d20 roll, regardless of modifiers or DC.
Cannot see. Attacks have disadvantage. Enemies have advantage on attacks against you.
Cannot move or take actions. Attacks against you automatically hit.
Cannot be seen. Attacks against you have disadvantage. Your attacks have advantage.
Turned to stone. Effectively dead until cured by magic.
Roll d20 + modifier vs a Difficulty Class (DC). Meet or beat the DC to succeed.
details:
The GM asks for a check when all three of the following are true:

  • The action has a negative consequence for failure
  • The action requires skill
  • There is time pressure

Roll 1d20 + relevant stat modifier against the Difficulty Class (DC).

Usually, you succeed at what you're trained to do without rolling. A wizard always reads magical runes; a thief always finds a trap if searching in the right area. If you take time to carefully examine something, you simply succeed.

Social encounters usually rely on what you say rather than Charisma checks.

definition: Easy = 9, Normal = 12, Hard = 15, Extreme = 18. The GM sets the DC based on the task's difficulty.
details:
The four standard DCs represent how difficult an action is:

DifficultyDCExamples
Easy9Leaping a narrow chasm, sneaking up on an inattentive guard
Normal12Kicking open a stuck door, picking a poor lock
Hard15Swimming against a strong current, giving first aid to stop dying
Extreme18Climbing a slippery cliff one-handed, restraining a frenzied lion

Contested Checks: When creatures work against each other, each rolls a relevant stat check simultaneously. Highest result wins (reroll ties).

definition: Awarded by the GM for exceptional roleplaying or heroism. Cash in to reroll any roll you just made. Must use new result. Max 1 per player.
details:
The GM awards luck tokens for exceptional roleplaying, heroism, or daring maneuvers — whether or not the action was successful.

  • Each player can hold only one luck token at a time
  • Cash in a luck token to reroll any roll you just made — you must use the new result
  • You can give your luck token to a companion

How many per session?

  • Pulpy/heroic: 2-3 new tokens per player per session
  • Grim/dark: The GM might not give out any

definition: Only roll when: the action has a negative consequence for failure, requires skill, and there is time pressure. Trained characters auto-succeed at routine tasks.
When two creatures contest each other, both roll. The higher result wins.
When traveling long distances, use initiative order. Check for random encounters based on area danger level.
In unfamiliar territory, the navigator makes an INT check when exiting a hex. Failure = lost, entering a random adjacent hex.
Unsafe: check every 3 hours. Risky: check every 2 hours. Deadly: check every hour.
Roll 1d6 x 10 minutes for remaining light. Rarely total darkness outside, even at night.
Between adventures, choose one downtime activity: Carousing, Learning, or other activities.
details:
Between adventures, you can choose to undertake one downtime activity. Downtime can last several days of in-game time, so the GM uses the Time Passes rule as needed.

Available downtime activities:

  • Carousing — Convert coin into XP, allies, and wild stories
  • Learning — Study a new skill with an instructor
  • Acts of Devotion — Serve your deity for blessings
  • Combat Training — Pit fighting for treasure and fame (Cursed Scroll 2)

The GM determines how much in-game time passes during a downtime activity.

definition: Convert treasure into XP by celebrating. Pay a cost, roll d8 + event bonus. Outcomes range from jail to legendary allies. Lasts several in-game days.
details:
When you return from the Shadowdark, you can carouse to celebrate your heroic exploits.

How it works: Each participant pitches in for the cost of the event. Then each participant rolls 1d8 + the event's bonus to determine their own outcome.

CostEventBonus
30 gpA worthy night of drinking and festivity+0
100 gpA full day and night of revelry and gambling+1
300 gpTwo days of crawling dozens of taverns+2
600 gpA three-day voyage into the finest food and drink+3
900 gpA hazy, weeklong bender+4
1,200 gpA ten-day fete that takes over a town+5
1,800 gpTwo legendary weeks that take over a city+6

Each character gains XP and other effects from their individual outcome roll. If the group plays out the results, the entire carousing group is usually present.

definition: 30 gp (+0), 100 gp (+1), 300 gp (+2), 600 gp (+3), 900 gp (+4), 1200 gp (+5), 1800 gp (+6). Higher investment = better outcomes.
details:
Roll 1d8 + event bonus to determine your carousing outcome:

RollOutcomeBenefit
1You wake up blearily in your bed2 XP
2Locked in stocks 1d4 days, fined 20% of wealth for arson2 XP
3Wake up in a gutter, 15% of wealth spent3 XP
4Donated 10% of wealth to a glib priest3 XP + priest ally
5Fined 10% of wealth for starting a tavern brawl3 XP + barred from tavern
6Thieves' Guild bilked you for 5% of wealth4 XP
7Led a tavern in an insulting song about a noble4 XP + bard ally
8Survived a blindfolded knife-throwing demo4 XP + luck token
9Beat a rival crawler in a test of skill5 XP + NPC ally or enemy
10Reflected a wizard's spell off your cup5 XP + luck token
11Pranked a despised corrupt merchant5 XP + City Watch ally
12Defeated a noble in a drinking contest5 XP + noble's debt
13Pulled off an ill-advised heist in a sorcerer's tower6 XP + treasure (80-100)
14+Wake up in the local ruler's stronghold holding a priceless heirloom. Footsteps approach...6 XP + treasure (90-100) if you escape

Wealth percentages are based on your total wealth at the time of carousing.

definition: Spend downtime learning a new skill. Requires a teacher, time, and gold. The GM determines specifics.
details:
Your character may wish to learn a new skill during downtime. You must find a capable instructor who is willing to teach you.

What you can learn:

  • A new language
  • Auxiliary skills (riding a sandworm, sailing, etc.)
  • New actions or advantage on certain checks

What you typically can't learn: Another class's or ancestry's unique talents.

Procedure:

  1. Work with the GM to determine what you can try to learn
  2. Make an Extreme (DC 18) Intelligence check
  3. Success: You learn the new skill
  4. Failure: You can try again as your next downtime activity, this time lowering the DC by one step (to Hard DC 15, then Normal DC 12, etc.)

Learning enables you to do new actions or gives you advantage on certain checks related to the skill.

definition: Devout PCs can use treasure to demonstrate faith instead of carousing. Invest 30-1800 gp in devotional events for XP and spiritual benefits.
details:
Priests and devout characters can spend downtime performing acts of devotion to their deity. This strengthens their divine connection and may earn blessings.

Types of devotion:

  • Temple Service: Assist at a temple, perform rites, counsel the faithful
  • Pilgrimage: Travel to a holy site relevant to your deity
  • Charitable Works: Aid the poor, heal the sick, protect the weak (for lawful deities)
  • Sacrifice: Destroy or donate valuables in your deity's name
  • Proselytizing: Spread your deity's teachings to new followers

The GM determines the outcome and any blessings earned. Devotion can also serve as penance to regain lost spells (see Penance rules).

definition: Scale from personal reflection (30 gp, +0) to a grand temple with permanent festival plaza (1800 gp, +6). Roll d8 + bonus for outcome.
details:
When performing acts of devotion, the GM may use devotional events to add flavor and consequences:

  • A divine vision or omen related to upcoming adventures
  • A test of faith that challenges the character's beliefs
  • An encounter with a rival or enemy of the deity
  • A blessing that grants a temporary boon (luck token, advantage on next check, etc.)
  • A request from the temple or faith community that leads to a side quest

Devotional events are entirely at the GM's discretion and should tie into the deity's themes and the campaign's narrative.

definition: Combat-focused PCs can use treasure to train instead of carousing. Invest 30-1800 gp for XP and martial benefits.
details:
Characters can spend downtime engaging in combat training, including pit fighting for treasure, XP, and fame.

Pit Fighting (Cursed Scroll 2: Red Sands)

PCs participate in organized bouts during downtime. The GM rolls for venue and stakes:

Danger LevelDescriptionLethality
LowLow stakes or safe venueFight to half HP or knockout
MidMid stakes or risky venueKnockout, rare death
HighHigh stakes or extreme venueFight to the death is common

Stakes (APL + 1d6):

RollStakesExample Rewards
2-5Low20 gp, small NPC favor, +2 carouse bonus
6-10Mid50 gp, big NPC favor, +3 carouse bonus
11-13High100 gp, royal favor, +4 carouse bonus
14+EpicGiant diamond, djinni wish, +5 carouse bonus

Venues (2d6): Shady back alley (2-4), cage fight (5-7), open-air arena (8-10), noble's private arena (11), glorious coliseum (12).

Twists (2d6): Additional danger like traps or banned equipment (2-5), none (6-9), donor increases stakes (10-11), attendee throws a boon (12).

Winners get the stakes and resulting XP. Killing humanoids is forbidden in most public venues.

definition: The referee who describes the world, controls NPCs and monsters, adjudicates rules, and drives the story. Also called the referee.
Shadowdark RPG emphasizes: player agency, emergent storytelling, real-time tension, resource management, and thinking outside the character sheet.
Crawling (dungeon exploration), Overland (hex travel), Combat, Downtime, and Roleplay. Each has slightly different rules emphasis.
Hidden hazards in the environment. Thieves can detect traps if searching the right area. Common types: pit, dart, poison needle, collapsing ceiling.
Environmental dangers: flooding, cave-ins, extreme temperatures, toxic gases. Usually require checks to avoid or survive.
A timed challenge where the party must overcome obstacles in sequence. Creates urgency and dramatic tension.
XP comes from treasure quality, not monster kills. This encourages stealth, negotiation, and creative problem-solving.
When players seem stuck or things slow down, roll on this table to inject an unexpected event into the scene.
Information hooks for adventures. Can be true, false, or partially true. Players gather rumors through NPC interactions.
Roll 2d6 + CHA mod: 0-6 Hostile, 7-8 Suspicious, 9 Neutral, 10-11 Curious, 12+ Friendly. Determines initial NPC attitude.
NPC adventuring parties that compete with the players. Can be allies, rivals, or enemies depending on circumstances.
Create monsters by assigning: Level, HP, AC, ATK bonus, damage, movement, alignment, and special abilities. Use the Monster Generator tables for quick creation.
Pre-built d100 tables for 20 different environments (Arctic, Cave, Desert, Forest, etc.). Each entry includes creatures, events, or situations.
An in-world dice gambling game using 3d6. Players bet coins on rolls of wizards (6s) and thieves (1s). Used for in-character gambling scenes.
Magic items have: Benefit (what it does), Bonus (numerical enhancement), Personality (sentient items), and sometimes Curse.
Some magic items are sentient with their own personality, goals, and alignment. They may resist wielders of opposing alignment.
Non-item rewards: titles, land grants, NPC allies, divine favor, or special abilities. Awarded for exceptional deeds.
